opening_quote.text: Until you make the unconscious conscious, it will direct your life and you will call it fate.

framing.opening_question: If we asked the five people closest to you to describe how you make decisions, would they all give the same answer?
framing.facilitator_says: D.I.S.C. isn't a label — it's a mirror. Today we're going to look at how you naturally show up, before we look at how you choose to show up.
framing.why_it_matters: Strategy starts with self-knowledge. You can't plan a life you don't understand. Your D.I.S.C. style shapes how you make decisions, who energises you, and what kind of work feels effortless. Knowing it doesn't lock you in — it gives you a starting point.
framing.closing_reflection: What is one D.I.S.C. trait that helps you, and one that gets in your way?
framing.bridge_to_next: Knowing your style is one filter. Now we look at the lenses you see through it: your attitudes.

reading_material.title: How To Read and Understand People — Bill Bonnstetter (TTI)
reading_material.content: A short primer on the D.I.S.C. behavioural model — the framework underneath this section. Bonnstetter explains why the four primary styles (Dominance, Influence, Steadiness, Compliance) are observable patterns, not fixed labels, and how each one tends to make decisions, communicate, and react under pressure. The PSP workshop adapts this work with permission.

exercises.title: D.I.S.C. — Discovering My Personal Behavioural Design
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.content:
The D.I.S.C. MODEL was developed by Bill Bonnstetter at Target Training International, Phoenix, Arizona. It is based on the psychology of Carl Jung and William Moulton Marston and is widely accepted as an effective tool for enhancing performance in all areas of life.

Through the combined Nature/Nurture process of growing, you have developed specific ways of interacting with the world. This is your Personal Behavioural Style, or CORE STYLE.

The D.I.S.C. model uses four factors to determine behaviour:

1. THE DOMINANCE FACTOR (D) — How you handle problems and challenges
2. THE INFLUENCE FACTOR (I) — How you handle people and influence others
3. THE STEADINESS FACTOR (S) — How you handle and pace yourself
4. THE COMPLIANCE FACTOR (C) — How you handle the rules set by others

How you will benefit:
• Increased Self Understanding
• Increased Understanding of Others
• Increased Communication Skills
• Less Stress
• Decreased Tension
• Improved Relationships (Family, Work/Business, Social)
content_json.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

exercises.title: EXERCISE: Core Style — Question 1 (Extroversion)
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.prompt: Question 1: Are you predominantly EXTROVERTED or INTROVERTED?

options.label: Extroverted — I gain energy from interacting with people and the outside world.
Introverted — I gain energy from quiet reflection and my inner world.

exercises.title: EXERCISE: Core Style — Question 2 (Orientation)
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.prompt: Question 2: Are you predominantly PEOPLE-ORIENTED or TASK-ORIENTED?

options.label: People-oriented — I focus on relationships, collaboration, and how people feel.
Task-oriented — I focus on results, structure, and getting things done.

exercises.title: Your Core Style
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.content:
Based on your two answers, your Core Style maps to one of the four D.I.S.C. types per the workbook's mapping rule:

1. Extroverted + Task-oriented = Core D — Dominance
2. Extroverted + People-oriented = Core I — Influence
3. Introverted + People-oriented = Core S — Steadiness
4. Introverted + Task-oriented = Core C — Compliance

{result}

On the next slides you'll read through each style's strengths, ideal environment, and comfort zones — not just your own. Knowing how other styles work makes every relationship easier.

exercises.title: HIGH D — Dominance Profile
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.content:
HIGH D — Dominance

If you are HIGH D, you are able to make decisions quickly, willing to state unpopular opinions, and naturally risk-taking. You are direct, decisive, and results-oriented.

Ideal environment
You thrive with freedom from controls, supervision and details. You prefer evaluation based on results — not process or method — and gravitate toward innovative, futuristic, non-routine work that gives you challenge and opportunity. You need a forum where you can express your ideas and viewpoints.

Characteristics
Ambitious, forceful, decisive, direct, independent, challenging, results-oriented. You carry a strong desire to win, work at a fast pace, and tend to juggle several things at once. You are quick to accept a challenge, are not afraid of high risk, and may interrupt or speak before thinking. Others sometimes find your style impatient or fear-inducing — useful to know when you collaborate.

Comfort zone with other styles
• With another D — the same approach, but your shared need to have your own way narrows the comfort zone.
• With an I — both extroverted; the I's verbal skill and people focus widens interaction comfortably.
• With an S — low comfort zone; the S tends to back down from your assertive style and both can feel frustrated.
• With a C — your need for immediate results clashes with the C's drive to analyse and test first.

exercises.title: HIGH I — Influence Profile
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.content:
HIGH I — Influence

If you are HIGH I, you are a natural optimist, trusting of others, and able to make others feel welcome and included. You are expressive, enthusiastic, and persuasive.

Ideal environment
You thrive on assignments with a high degree of people contact — motivating groups, building networks. You prefer a democratic supervisor you can associate with, freedom from control and detail, freedom of movement, and multi-changing work tasks.

Characteristics
Expressive, enthusiastic, friendly, demonstrative, talkative, stimulating. You have a good sense of humour, treat everyone as a friend, are fun-loving and a creative problem solver. You are usually optimistic, may talk before thinking, sometimes lose track of time, prefer to back away from conflict, can tend toward disorganisation, and are very trusting of others.

Comfort zone with other styles
• With a D — both extroverted; your verbal skills and people focus widen the comfort zone.
• With another I — high comfort zone, maximised by mutual enjoyment of personal interaction.
• With an S — both people-oriented, but the S's social conscience may irritate you, and the S may perceive you as unfeeling.
• With a C — your people skills and high trust threshold relax the C's need to establish rationale for trust.

exercises.title: HIGH S — Steadiness Profile
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.content:
HIGH S — Steadiness

If you are HIGH S, you have a tenacity for order and a natural ability to organize tasks. You are steady, reliable, and calming to those around you.

Ideal environment
You thrive in jobs where standards and methods are established, where long-standing relationships develop, and where you receive personal attention and recognition for tasks completed well. You prefer a stable, predictable environment that allows time for change, and where people can be dealt with on a personal, intimate basis.

Characteristics
Methodical, systematic, reliable, steady, relaxed, modest. You need secure situations, are a good planner, need closure, and listen well. You usually stabilize others, may mask your emotions, tend to be indirect to avoid conflict, can be possessive of things, prefer low risk, occasionally hold a grudge, adapt quickly to others, and tend to resist changes.

Comfort zone with other styles
• With a D — low comfort zone; you tend to back down from the D's assertive style and both can feel frustrated.
• With an I — both people-oriented, but your social conscience may irritate the I, and you may perceive the I as unfeeling.
• With another S — very high comfort zone; similar opinions plus high tolerance and sociability.
• With a C — your high respect for others and tolerance of differences let you approach a C without alarming their introverted response.

exercises.title: HIGH C — Compliance Profile
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.content:
HIGH C — Compliance

If you are HIGH C, you are a natural systems developer, a good quality-control person, and willing to dig for information. You are analytical, careful, and precise.

Ideal environment
You thrive where critical thinking is needed and rewarded, where assignments can be followed through to completion, in technical, task-oriented, specialized work. You prefer environments where noise and people are at a minimum, with close relationships among a small group, and where quality and standards matter.

Characteristics
Analytical, contemplative, conservative, exacting, careful, deliberate. You like to organize and analyse, work well alone, hold high expectations, follow rules, are self-competitive, and can solve complex problems. You like as much data as possible, are hard on yourself, never take unnecessary chances, may see emotions as irrational, tend to see faults in others, and can analyse things to death.

Comfort zone with other styles
• With a D — the D's need for immediate results clashes with your drive to analyse first.
• With an I — the I's people skills and high trust threshold relax your need to establish rationale for trust.
• With an S — the S's respect for others and tolerance of differences let them approach you without alarming your introverted response.
• With another C — high comfort zone; mutual respect for rules and protocol plus a shared desire to avoid conflict.

content_json.prompt:
Based on the D.I.S.C. exercise above, what is your Core Style? Use the two-question method to confirm: (1) Am I predominantly EXTROVERTED or INTROVERTED? (2) Am I predominantly PEOPLE-ORIENTED or TASK-ORIENTED?

Extroverted + Task-Oriented = Core D | Extroverted + People-Oriented = Core I | Introverted + People-Oriented = Core S | Introverted + Task-Oriented = Core C

Write your Core Style and reflect on how it shows up in your daily life:

exercises.title: Characteristics of Your Core Style
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.content: Answer the two questions on the quiz slide to see your matched style's characteristics here.

sections_by_style.D:
If you are HIGH D, you are:
• Able to make decisions quickly
• Willing to state unpopular opinions
• Risk taking
sections_by_style.I:
If you are HIGH I, you:
• Are a natural optimist
• Trusting of others
• Able to make others feel welcome and/or included
sections_by_style.S:
If you are HIGH S, you:
• Have a tenacity for order
• Possess a natural ability to organize tasks
sections_by_style.C:
If you are HIGH C, you:
• Are a natural systems developer
• Are a good quality control person
• Are willing to dig for information

exercises.title: Your Ideal Environment
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.content: Answer the two questions on the quiz slide to see your matched style's ideal environment here.

sections_by_style.D:
Ideal environment for the HIGH D:
• Freedom from controls, supervision and details
• Evaluation based on results, not process or method
• An innovative and futuristic oriented environment
• Non-routine work with challenge and opportunity
• A forum for them to express their ideas and viewpoints
sections_by_style.I:
Ideal environment for the HIGH I:
• Assignments with a high degree of people contacts
• Tasks involving motivating groups and establishing a network of contacts
• Democratic supervisor with whom they can associate
• Freedom from control and detail
• Freedom of movement
• Multi-changing work tasks
sections_by_style.S:
Ideal environment for the HIGH S:
• Jobs for which standards and methods are established
• Environment where long standing relationships can be or are developed
• Personal attention and recognition for tasks complete and well done
• Stable and predictable environment
• Environment that allows time for change
• Environment where people can be dealt with on a personal, intimate basis
sections_by_style.C:
Ideal environment for the HIGH C:
• Where critical thinking is needed and rewarded
• Assignments can be followed through to completion
• Technical, task-oriented work, specialized
• Noise and people are at a minimum
• Close relationship with a small group of people
• Environment where quality and/or standards are important

exercises.title: Your Comfort Zones
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.content: Answer the two questions on the quiz slide to see how your matched style interacts with each of the others.

sections_by_style.D:
Comfort Zones for HIGH D:
• D ↔ D: Though both have the same approach toward life, their tendency to challenge and need to have their own way reduces their Comfort Zone.
• D ↔ I: Both are extroverted. I's verbal skills and people orientation allow a wider Comfort Zone for interaction.
• D ↔ S: Low Comfort Zone due to dissimilar personalities. S's tendency to back down from D's strong assertive style frustrates both parties.
• D ↔ C: D's need for immediate results clashes with C's drive to analyse and test the water first.
sections_by_style.I:
Comfort Zones for HIGH I:
• I ↔ D: Both are extroverted. I's verbal skills and people orientation allow a wider Comfort Zone for interaction.
• I ↔ I: High Comfort Zone is maximized by their enjoyment of and need for personal interaction.
• I ↔ S: Though both are people oriented, S's social conscience may irritate I, and S may perceive I as unfeeling.
• I ↔ C: I's people skills and their high trust threshold relaxes C's need to establish rationale for trust.
sections_by_style.S:
Comfort Zones for HIGH S:
• S ↔ D: Low Comfort Zone due to dissimilar personalities. S's tendency to back down from D's strong assertive style frustrates both parties.
• S ↔ I: Though both are people oriented, S's social conscience may irritate I, and S may perceive I as unfeeling.
• S ↔ S: Similar opinions and their high tolerance and sociability create a very high Comfort Zone.
• S ↔ C: S's high respect for others and tolerance of differences allow them to approach C without alarming C's introverted response.
sections_by_style.C:
Comfort Zones for HIGH C:
• C ↔ D: D's need for immediate results clashes with C's drive to analyse and test the water first.
• C ↔ I: I's people skills and their high trust threshold relaxes C's need to establish rationale for trust.
• C ↔ S: S's high respect for others and tolerance of differences allow them to approach C without alarming C's introverted response.
• C ↔ C: Respect for rules and established protocol and their desire to avoid conflict create a High Comfort Zone.

opening_quote.text: Between stimulus and response there is a space. In that space is our power to choose our response.

framing.opening_question: What is one belief you hold about yourself that you have never actually questioned?
framing.facilitator_says: Attitudes are the lenses we look through. We rarely notice them — but they shape every decision we make.
framing.why_it_matters: Even with the right personality and the right skills, the wrong attitude will sabotage you. The good news: attitudes can be examined, named, and chosen. We're going to surface yours so you can decide which to keep, which to soften, and which to retire.
framing.closing_reflection: Which one attitude — if you changed it tomorrow — would change the most about your life?
framing.bridge_to_next: Attitudes are HOW you see. Values are WHAT you protect. Let's go there next.

reading_material.title: Man's Search for Meaning — Viktor Frankl
reading_material.content: The book the opening quote comes from. Frankl, a psychiatrist who survived the Nazi camps, argues that meaning is not given to us — it is the response we choose to whatever we are handed. For this section, the relevant move is his: you cannot always change the stimulus, but the space between stimulus and response is where your attitude lives. Naming that space is the first step in this exercise.

exercises.title: What Is an Attitude?
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.content:
In 1928, Eduard Spranger wrote and published The Types of Men. Spranger observed that all people view the world differently. Whenever several people viewed the same object or event, they invariably placed different values upon it. Through his research, Spranger identified 6 world views that affect the values people place upon objects or events. He labelled these views as Attitudes.

An attitude is:
1. A world view — a way of looking at life
2. A paradigm of thought
3. A value or judgment of life
4. A mindset

Spranger noted that:
• VALUES AND ATTITUDES → create → WANTS
• WANTS → determine → BEHAVIOUR
• BEHAVIOUR → achieves → RESULTS

We all see the world through our own attitude-tinted eyes. And we either reject or value various aspects of life based upon our attitude towards them. According to Spranger, there is no such thing as a 'good' or 'bad' attitude.
content_json.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

exercises.title: EXERCISE: Identifying Your Attitudes
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with permission from How To Read and Understand People Copyright 1988 Target Training International)

content_json.prompt:
From the following list of likes and dislikes, select those that most accurately describe who you are. The first answer that comes to your mind is usually a good indication of how you truly feel.

Group W (Theoretical — Questions 1–9):
Group A (Aesthetic — Questions 10–18):
Group T (Traditional — Questions 19–25):
Group U (Utilitarian — Questions 26–35):
Group S (Social — Questions 36–42):
Group I (Individualistic — Questions 43–50):

Check all that apply, then count your totals per group to find your top two attitudes.

options.label: I like solving problems.
I like objectivity in all areas of life.
I like identifying, differentiating, generalizing, systematizing.
I like intellectual process.
I like discovery, understanding, ordering.
I like pursuit of knowledge, identifying truth and untruth.
I like knowledge for the sake of knowing.
I hate inability to know, learn or discover.
I hate emotional, subjective experience without rational justification.
I like appreciation of form, harmony and beauty.
I like enjoyment of all the senses.
I like subjective experience.
I like understanding the feelings of myself and others.
I like self-realization, self-fulfilment and self-actualization.
I like creative expression.
I like appreciation of impressions.
I hate disturbances in form, beauty and harmony in myself, others and/or the environment.
I object to objective truth.
I like understanding the totality of life.
I like finding meaning in life.
I like pursuit of the divine in life.
I like following and dying for a cause.
I like living constantly according to a 'closed' book.
I like converting others to my way of thinking.
I don't like people who oppose my beliefs.
I like practicality in all areas of my life.
I like surpassing others in the attainment of wealth.
I like using resources to accomplish results.
I like gaining a measurable return on all my investments.
I like creative application of resources.
I like producing goods, materials, services and marketing them for economic gain.
I like capitalism.
I like maximizing or conserving resources.
I don't like to see wasted resources, time, materials and/or services.
I hate investments with inadequate or no return.
I like investing in others.
I like selflessness.
I like generosity of time, talent and resources.
I like seeing and developing potential in others.
I like championing a worthy cause.
I don't like rampant individualism.
I hate decisions and actions which are insensitive to people's needs.
I like leading others.
I like achieving position.
I like advancing position and strategic alliances.
I like attaining and using power to accomplish purpose.
I like planning and carrying out a winning strategy.
I like tactics and positioning.
I hate actual or perceived threat of diminished power or position.
I hate inability to advance, lack of opportunity for individual advancement.

content_json.prompt: Your prefilled order below is derived from the checklist you just completed. The count beside each item is auto-calculated and read-only.

items.label: W — The Theoretical Attitude: I use my intellect to systematically understand and discover the truth. The discovery of Truth and Knowledge is my main motivation in life. I seek knowledge for its own sake.
A — The Aesthetic Attitude: I enjoy and experience the beauty around me and find meaning and fulfilment within the experience of living. My main motivation is to experience the Inner Vision.
T — The Traditional Attitude: I pursue and understand the highest meaning of life within an established system of beliefs. My main motivation is to search and find the highest value in life.
U — The Utilitarian Attitude: I see most things in terms of their usefulness and practicality. My main motivation is to turn what I do and have into a useful purpose.

S — The Social Attitude: I enjoy helping others and believe that all decisions should be sensitive to people's needs. My main goal in life is to do what I can to eliminate hate and conflict in the world.
I — The Individualistic Attitude: I want to achieve the highest position possible and wield the greatest power in my chosen field and will do everything within my ability to reach these goals.

content_json.prompt:
Reflect on the following Attitude Power Points and write how your top two attitudes show up in your daily life, career choices, and relationships:

1. An attitude is a way of valuing life — a world view, a paradigm of thought and action.
2. Most of a person's choices throughout life are guided by the hierarchy of their attitudes and needs satisfactions.
3. Attitudes determine our purpose and direction in life, stimulating us to action.
4. Attitudes are relatively constant throughout life and will usually change only in relation to a Significant Emotional Event.
5. Behaviour (D.I.S.C.) is the methodology for fulfilling the passions driven by our attitudes.
6. Attitudes tend to interact with one another.

How do your top two attitude types interact? Do they support or conflict with each other?
content_json.placeholder: My top two attitudes are ___ and ___. I notice them showing up in my life when...

opening_quote.text: It's not hard to make decisions when you know what your values are.

framing.opening_question: If you had to give up everything you own except five things — and not material things, but five values — what would you protect?
framing.facilitator_says: Values aren't what we say we believe. They are what we spend on — time, energy, attention. Today we make that visible.
framing.why_it_matters: Your values are your compass. The Goal Setting section later in this course only works if you have named which direction is north. Most people inherit their values from family, school, or workplace and never audit them. The Values Shopping Spree exercise is your audit.
framing.closing_reflection: Which of your top five values is currently underfed?
framing.bridge_to_next: You know your style, your lenses, your compass. Now: what hats are you actually wearing day-to-day?

reading_material.title: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People — Stephen R. Covey
reading_material.content: Covey's framework treats values as the centre of a personal mission and warns about the cost of confusing values with urgencies. Habit 2 — Begin with the end in mind — is the lens to bring into the Shopping Spree exercise: you are not buying what feels nice today, you are buying the things that, ten years from now, you would still defend as worth the money. Covey also separates principles (universal) from values (chosen) — useful vocabulary if you find the exercise hard.

content_json.prompt:
CONGRATULATIONS! You have won an imaginary $100,000! But there's a catch — you have to spend it all in the PSP™ Value Boutique. Read through the complete list carefully, then allocate dollar amounts to the items that are important to you. You MUST spend all of the $100,000 — no more, no less.

Guidelines:
• Buy only those things most important to YOU — not what others say should be important.
• Pretend you are starting with nothing.
• Do not let parents, priests, friends or teachers influence your purchases.
• Read through the ENTIRE list before purchasing.
items: A process guaranteed to rid the world of prejudice and racism.
An opportunity to help the sick and impoverished and allow them happier lives.
The charisma and resources necessary to become famous (movie star, sports hero, astronaut, author).
The chance to create and implement a sure-fire money-making idea.
A morning massage everyday and breakfast, lunch and dinner cooked by a world class chef.
Absolute enlightenment and understanding of myself.
Consistency throughout my life between thought, word and deed.
The opportunity to be my own boss.
As much money as Bill Gates (even more).
To be President of The World.
The perfect relationship.
My dream house with a breath-taking view, filled with my favorite things.
To be one of the most attractive people in the world.
To be perfectly healthy.
Peace of mind.
My own library filled with every publication I ever wanted to read.
Absolute union with the Almighty in thought and deed.
A world of equal justice and opportunity for everyone.
Enough money to fund an organization that truly helps everyone.
To win the Nobel Prize in my chosen field, and be praised everywhere in the world.
To master every skill needed to be the best in my job.
A genie at my beck and call, with unlimited power to satisfy my every wish so I can enjoy life to the fullest.
To be the wisest person ever to live.
A bottle of 'honesty gas' large enough to cure the whole world of lying.
To be completely independent of anyone.
To own a Texas oil well.
To be a role model and guru to people throughout the world.
To perfectly express my love and caring.
A Super Pass that lets me into any concert, play, opera, art show or ballet and to travel anywhere I want by land, sea or air.
A complete make-over: hair, clothes, manicure, plastic surgery, time at a spa, whatever I want in order to look my best.
The perfect body.
Absolute self-esteem and the confidence to think and say what I feel.
To learn anything instantly and be able to recall it at a moment's notice.
Uninterrupted conversations with any spiritual leader(s) I want, past or present.

content_json.prompt: After completing the Values Shopping Spree, add each pair of items to total the score for that value. Then rank the 17 values from highest to lowest total spending. Your top 3 are your core values.

items.label: Justice — Moral rightness, honor, fairness (Items 1 + 18)
Humanitarianism — Devotion to the promotion of human welfare (Items 2 + 19)
Recognition — Acknowledgment of one's significance or importance (Items 3 + 20)
Achievement — Accomplishment (Items 4 + 21)
Pleasure — Enjoyment, satisfaction, satiation (Items 5 + 22)
Wisdom — Understanding of what is true, right or lasting (Items 6 + 23)
Honesty — Straightforwardness, integrity, consistency (Items 7 + 24)
Autonomy — Independence, self-containment (Items 8 + 25)
Economy — Abundance of material possessions, wealth (Items 9 + 26)
Power — Control, authority or influence (Items 10 + 27)
Love — Affection, warm attachment, caring (Items 11 + 28)
Aesthetics — Appreciation and enjoyment of beauty for beauty's sake (Items 12 + 29)
Physical Attractiveness — Concern for the appearance of one's body (Items 13 + 30)
Health — Concern for the health of one's body (Items 14 + 31)
Feeling of Well-Being — Freedom from anxiety, imbalance; peace of mind (Items 15 + 32)
Knowledge — Seeking of truth and/or information (Items 16 + 33)
Spiritual Well-Being — Communion with, obedience to, and activity on behalf of a Supreme Being (Items 17 + 34)

content_json.intro: Based on the Values Shopping Spree exercise, list your top three values (those you spent the most money on) and reflect on how each one shows up as a driving force in your life. How do these values influence your career choices, relationships, and daily decisions?

questions.prompt: My #1 top value is ___ — describe how it shows up as a driving force in your life, career, and relationships.
My #2 top value is ___ — describe how it shows up as a driving force in your life, career, and relationships.
My #3 top value is ___ — describe how it shows up as a driving force in your life, career, and relationships.

opening_quote.text: You can do anything, but not everything.

framing.opening_question: If someone made a documentary of your last 30 days, how many different characters would you play in it?
framing.facilitator_says: We all wear multiple hats. The question isn't whether — it's whether each hat is one we still want to wear, or one we just inherited.
framing.why_it_matters: Roles consume your hours. If you don't audit them, they will consume your life. The Life Line and Past Experience Inventory in this section help you see the shape of your time, not just the content of it. That shape is what your goals will have to fit inside.
framing.closing_reflection: Which role on your list deserves more time than it currently gets — and which one secretly deserves less?
framing.bridge_to_next: Roles are demands on your time. Skills are what travel with you through every role. Let's catalogue them.

reading_material.title: Essentialism: The Disciplined Pursuit of Less — Greg McKeown
reading_material.content: McKeown's central move is to treat your roles as decisions, not obligations. A role inherited from family, employer, or habit is still a role you renew every day by showing up. The book offers a useful filter — 'if it isn't a clear yes, it's a clear no' — for the Life Line exercise. Read this before deciding which roles to keep on the list and which to retire.

exercises.title: EXERCISE: My Life Line — Past, Present and Future

content_json.prompt:
Draw a line (in your workbook or mind) that depicts your life as you see it from beginning to end. Your lifeline may be any shape and go in any direction — a road, river, thread, path, or graph line. Place three marks indicating your birth, your death, and where you are now.

Once you have reflected on your lifeline, write your observations below:

THE PAST (your unique experiences, acquired skills, and personal history):
• The past has happened; we cannot change it.
• Your past has a place in your current life — accept it, reconstruct it, evaluate it creatively.
• The past can yield insight into your unfulfilled potential.

THE PRESENT (the here and now with all its joys, frustrations, priorities and demands):
• You can choose where you want to go based on what satisfies you now.
• You have undeveloped potential that suggests directions for future development.

THE FUTURE (your aspirations, dreams, goals, hopes and fears):
• To a large degree, you create your own future.
• All three perspectives are necessary to develop a more fulfilling life plan.

What themes do you notice across your past, present, and future?
content_json.placeholder:
Looking at my lifeline, I notice...

In my PAST, the most significant experiences were...

In my PRESENT, I am...

For my FUTURE, I hope/fear...

content_json.prompt: Write 10 separate short statements that answer the question 'Who am I?' Then rank these statements according to their importance to YOU — not how you think others would rank them. Ensure that at least 3–4 of your roles are defined in view of your responsibilities or accountabilities to specific individuals or groups (family, children, spouses, etc.).
headers: Who Am I? (complete the sentence: I am...)

questions.prompt: Who have been the most influential people in your life and in what way?
What were the critical incidents (events) that made you who you are?
What have been the major interests in your life so far?
What were the significant experiences in your school or college career?
What were your significant work experiences?
What were the most significant decisions in your life?
What makes you alive and excited? What events or activities spur you on?
What am I really good at? What strengths do I have to build on?
What do I do poorly? What do I need to develop or correct?
What do I want to stop doing that affects my effectiveness?
What do I want to change in my life?
What do I want to start doing much better?
What do I want to learn or develop in myself?
Who are the persons or organizations that can help me achieve my goals?

content_json.intro: Answer each of the following 14 questions as honestly and fully as possible. This inventory helps you identify the experiences, influences, and patterns that have shaped who you are today.

exercises.title: EXERCISE: A Contract With Myself
exercises.attribution: (From Professor Muriel James, author of Born to Win, Breaking Free, Passion for Life — on transactional analysis)

questions.prompt: Article 1 — What do I want to change in my life for the better?
Article 2 — What do I need to get what I want?
Article 3 — What am I willing to do?
Article 4 — How will I measure my success?
Article 5 — How could I sabotage myself from achieving success?
Article 6 — What are my next positive steps toward change?

content_json.intro: Complete this personal contract for change (as suggested by Professor Muriel James, author of Born to Win):

questions.prompt: My Vision / Mission / Purpose for this life is...
In relation to MYSELF...
In relation to OTHERS...
In relation to THE WORLD...
If I put it in one sentence, my mission is...

content_json.intro: A Mission/Vision Statement gives you a powerful tool to help you focus and prioritize your life. Tip: look at your own eyes in the mirror and repeat each line back to yourself. Does it fit you completely?

opening_quote.text: Your work is going to fill a large part of your life, and the only way to be truly satisfied is to do what you believe is great work.

framing.opening_question: What is something you do well that other people seem to find difficult?
framing.facilitator_says: Skills aren't just job descriptions. They are patterns — and patterns travel with you wherever you go.
framing.why_it_matters: Knowing your transferable skills means you are never trapped in one role. They are leverage. They are the answer to 'what could I do if I had to start over tomorrow?' Strategy needs options, and your skills inventory is your option set.
framing.closing_reflection: Which skill, if developed, would unlock the most doors for you next year?
framing.bridge_to_next: You have your style, lenses, compass, hats, and toolkit. Now we put it all together — into goals.

reading_material.title: What Color Is Your Parachute? — Richard N. Bolles
reading_material.content: Bolles invented the modern transferable-skills inventory. The book's core exercise — the Flower — separates a skill from the job that taught you the skill, so you can carry it into completely different work. Read the chapter on transferable skills before the inventory exercise; it will help you list skills as verbs (analyse, listen, organise) rather than as job titles.

exercises.title: EXERCISE: Transferable Skills — Dealing with Individuals

content_json.prompt: Rate each skill on a scale of 1 (lowest) to 5 (highest). These are skills you use when dealing with individuals one-on-one.

items.label: Take instructions, Serve or Help
Diagnose, Treat or Heal
Do well in conversation, in person, or on the phone
Refer people, or help two to link up
Communicate well in writing (e.g. excellent letters)
Assess, Evaluate, Screen, or Select Individuals
Instruct, Teach, Tutor, or Train Individuals
Persuade, Motivate, Recruit, Sell to Individuals
Advise, Coach, Counsel, Mentor
Represent Others, Interpret Others' Ideas

exercises.title: EXERCISE: Transferable Skills — Dealing with Groups

content_json.prompt: Rate each skill on a scale of 1 (lowest) to 5 (highest). These are skills you use when working with groups and audiences.

items.label: Communicate Effectively to a group or a multitude
Play games or lead others in recreation
Manage, Supervise or Run (a business, fund drive etc.)
Use words expressively in speaking or writing
Teach, Train, or Design educational events
Follow through getting things done
Make presentations in person, or on TV or film
Guide a Group Discussion conveying warmth
Take the lead, be a pioneer
Perform, Entertain, Amuse or Inspire
Persuade a Group, Motivate or Sell
Initiating, Starting Up, Founding, Or Establishing
Sign, Mimic, Act, Sing, or Play an Instrument
Consult, give advice to groups in your area of service
Negotiate between two parties, or resolve conflict

exercises.title: EXERCISE: Transferable Skills — Dealing with Information & Data

content_json.prompt: Rate each skill on a scale of 1 (lowest) to 5 (highest). These are skills you use when gathering, managing, storing and retrieving information, data, and ideas.

items.label: Compile, Search, or Research
Gather information by interviewing or observing people
Gather information by studying or observing things
Have an acute sense of hearing, smell, taste, or sight
Imagine, invent, create or design new ideas
Copy and/or compare similarities or differences
Compute, working with numbers, do accounting
Analyze, break down into its parts
Organize, classify, systematize, and/or prioritize
Plan, lay out step by step process for achieving a goal
Adapt, translate (incl. computer programming), develop or improve
Visualize, draw, paint, dramatize, create videos or software
Synthesize, combining parts into a whole
Problem solve or see patterns among a mass of data
Decide, evaluate, appraise, or make recommendations
Keep Records (Incl. on film or on a computer)
Store or File: microfiche, video, audio, or computer
Retrieve information, ideas, data
Enable other people to find or retrieve information
Have a superior memory, keeping track of details

content_json.intro: List your top six favorite AND strongest skills. Remember — favorite AND strongest, not just strongest. Feel free to add any skills not found in the previous exercise. Your passions matter here as much as your proficiency.

questions.prompt: My favorite and strongest skill is:
My second favorite and strongest skill is:
My third favorite and strongest skill is:
My fourth favorite and strongest skill is:
My fifth favorite and strongest skill is:
My sixth favorite and strongest skill is:

opening_quote.text: A goal without a plan is just a wish.

framing.opening_question: If you looked back on your life ten years from now and everything had gone well — what would need to be true?
framing.facilitator_says: Most people have wishes. Fewer have goals. The difference is specificity, a deadline, and honesty about difficulty. Right now we are going to take your most important aims — across every area of life, not just the obvious ones — and put them on paper.
framing.why_it_matters: People who write down specific goals achieve significantly more than those who don't — not because writing is magic, but because it forces clarity. A vague aspiration like 'be more successful' is not a goal; it's a direction. This inventory asks you to be precise, to assess how important each goal really is, and to notice where your goals might conflict with each other.
framing.closing_reflection: Which goal — if you achieved nothing else this year — would make the biggest difference to your life?
framing.bridge_to_next: You have named your goals. Now we are going to pressure-test them against each other, because not all goals can be pursued at the same time.

reading_material.title: Goals! — Brian Tracy
reading_material.content: Tracy's central claim is that fewer than 3% of people have written, specific goals with deadlines — and that this single habit accounts for more of the variance in outcomes than talent, education, or circumstance. He draws on research from goal-setting psychology to show that clarity of intention is the primary differentiator between high and average performers. Read the first two chapters before the Life Goal Inventory; they will help you move from abstract wishes to concrete, dated targets.

exercises.title: EXERCISE: My Life Goal Inventory
exercises.attribution: (Adapted with kind permission from Dr. David Kolb. From Organizational Behavior: An Experiential Approach, Fifth Edition — David Kolb, Irwin M. Rubin and Joyce Osland. Prentice Hall, Englewood Cliffs, N.Y. 07632.)

content_json.prompt:
Describe your aims and goals for ALL areas of your life as fully as possible. Be as specific as you can — include the date by which you wish to have each goal achieved. Sift each goal through your four filters: Personal Style, Attitudes and Values, Roles and their Demands, and Transferable Skills.

Goal Importance: H (High) = very important compared to others | M (Moderate) = moderately important | L (Low) = other goals are more important

Ease of Attainment: H (High) = easily reachable | M (Moderate) = reachable with moderate difficulty | L (Low) = very difficult to reach

Categories:
A — Career Satisfaction: your goals for your future job or career, including specific positions you want to hold.
B — Status and Respect: how important is receiving respect from others? From whom? What groups do you belong to?
C — Personal Relationships: goals in your relationships with colleagues, parents, friends, people in general.
D — Learning and Education: what would you like to know more about? What skills do you want to develop?
E — Material Rewards and Possessions: what kind of wealth is important to you? What possessions would you want?
F — Leisure Satisfaction: goals for leisure time — hobbies, sports, vacations, interests you want to develop.
G — Spiritual Growth and Religion: goals for peace of mind, your search for meaning — relationship to nature, community, devotional life, meditation.
H — Open: goals that don't fit the above categories.
Your goals from the Life Goal Inventory appear below. Drag them to rank by importance — most important at the top. Your top 10 are recorded.

opening_quote.text: The essence of strategy is choosing what not to do.

framing.opening_question: If you could only work on one goal for the next six months, which one would move the most other goals forward?
framing.facilitator_says: Not all goals are created equal — and some actively get in each other's way. This matrix is where strategy gets real: you are going to map exactly which of your goals support each other and which ones create friction.
framing.why_it_matters: Most goal-setting fails because people pursue everything at once. The Cross-Impact Matrix forces you to see your goals as a system — which ones are multipliers, which create drag, and where the real conflicts lie. Once you can see that map, your priorities stop being guesses.
framing.closing_reflection: Which two goals, if achieved together, would create the most momentum across the rest of your list?
framing.bridge_to_next: You have mapped the logic of your goals. Now we move from analysis to imagination — seeing yourself already there.

reading_material.title: Essentialism: The Disciplined Pursuit of Less — Greg McKeown
reading_material.content: McKeown's argument is that most people spread effort across too many things and achieve mediocrity everywhere, when disciplined focus on fewer things produces exceptional results. The Cross-Impact Matrix is the analytical version of his central question: 'What is the one thing that, by doing it, makes everything else easier or unnecessary?' Read Chapter 8 on Trade-offs — it directly maps onto what you are about to do.

content_json.prompt:
One of the major blocks to goal accomplishment is the conflict between goals. Take your top 8 goals and enter them across the top and down the left-hand side. Then use the following symbols to rank the effect each goal has on the others:

+ = helpful impact (working on Goal 1 will HELP me with Goal 3)
- = hindering impact (working on Goal 2 will make it MORE DIFFICULT to accomplish Goal 5)
0 = no impact of any kind

Note: Where a goal meets itself, mark 0 (obviously Goal 1 has no impact where it meets Goal 1).

After completing the matrix, list at least five conflicts in order of importance.

opening_quote.text: Imagination is everything. It is the preview of life's coming attractions.

framing.opening_question: Can you close your eyes and see yourself — in vivid, sensory detail — already living your most important goal?
framing.facilitator_says: Strategy is not just analysis. The brain cannot fully distinguish between a vividly imagined experience and a real one — which means your imagination is a training ground. We are going to use it.
framing.why_it_matters: Olympic athletes have used mental rehearsal for decades. Neuroscience confirms why: visualising an action activates the same neural pathways as performing it. When you picture yourself succeeding — specifically, emotionally, sensorially — you are reprogramming your baseline expectation of what is possible. That shift in expectation changes behaviour.
framing.closing_reflection: What did you see clearly — and what was still fuzzy or hard to hold? What does that tell you?
framing.bridge_to_next: You have seen the destination. Now we identify what stands between here and there — and build the plan to clear it.

reading_material.title: Psycho-Cybernetics — Maxwell Maltz
reading_material.content: Maltz, a plastic surgeon who noticed that some patients' self-image did not change after surgery, developed a model of the mind as a goal-seeking servo-mechanism. His central insight: the subconscious cannot distinguish between a real and a vividly imagined experience. His visualisation exercises predate the sports psychology literature by decades and remain among the most practical available. Read Part One before the journal exercise.

exercises.title: The Importance of Visualization
exercises.attribution: Personal Strategic Planning™ — © Sam Koshy / Compass Career Life Solutions, Canada.

content_json.content:
Research has shown that every thought we think has an affect on the chemistry of our bodies. By visualizing goal attainment, we actually increase our motivation to achieve them.

**The next exercise will show you how visualization will help you in achieving your goals:**

- Stand up.
- Take a deep breath and let it out slowly.
- Repeat two or three times. If you know other body relaxation exercises, do them now.
- Once you are relaxed, close your eyes, get comfortable, and go to a place called the "hallways of your mind". This is a limitless place with an infinite number of rooms and spaces. Can you see it? Find the place that has to do with achieving all 8 of your goals.
- Visualize yourself having achieved all 8 of these goals.
- Take note of your posture in this situation. Note your breathing pattern and how your muscles feel. Are they tense, relaxed, alert?
- Take note if there is anyone else with you in this place. Who are they? What are their posture and facial expressions? How are they relating to you?
- Note everything around you in this place, including colours, sounds, textures, smells and tastes. Recognize that you can go to this place any time. And that you can change anything at any time.
- Now take a deep breath and blow it out as if you were blowing out a candle.
- Open your eyes and take a moment to reorientate yourself to where you are.

Do this exercise at least once a day, twice if you can. The best times are in the morning when you first wake up and at night as you're dozing off to sleep. Go to this place any time you are under stress or have lost track of where you're going and what you are doing in your life. Some find it helpful to use a picture that represents their future achievements. Whether you buy one or draw one yourself, a concrete visual representation can be very powerful.

exercises.title: Visualization Journal
exercises.attribution: Personal Strategic Planning™ — © Sam Koshy / Compass Career Life Solutions, Canada.

content_json.intro: After completing the visualization practice above, record what you experienced. Return to this journal each time you repeat the practice — your answers will deepen and shift over the weeks ahead.

questions.prompt: What did you see in the place that has to do with achieving all 8 of your goals? Describe the scene as vividly as you can.
Who else was with you in this place? What were their posture and facial expressions, and how were they relating to you?
Note everything around you — colours, sounds, textures, smells, tastes. What stood out?
Name one concrete action you can take this week, drawn from what you saw, that moves you closer to your future self.

opening_quote.text: It is not the mountain we conquer, but ourselves.

framing.opening_question: What is the one obstacle — inside you, not outside you — that most reliably stops you from following through?
framing.facilitator_says: This is where the work lands. Everything you have done — understanding your style, examining your attitudes, naming your values, mapping your roles, listing your skills, setting your goals — all of it comes to nothing without action. This section is about closing the gap.
framing.why_it_matters: Goal failure almost always traces to the same causes: vague plans, unexamined obstacles, and unchallenged alibis. This section attacks all three. You will plan specifically, anticipate honestly, and — in the Declaration of Self-Esteem — remind yourself of who you are before the obstacles tried to convince you otherwise.
framing.closing_reflection: What one action will you take in the next 48 hours that moves you toward your most important goal?

reading_material.title: The War of Art — Steven Pressfield
reading_material.content: Pressfield names the force that stops meaningful work 'Resistance' — and argues it is always proportional to the importance of the work. The more meaningful the goal, the stronger the Resistance. His book is a short, direct account of what it takes to act anyway. Read it alongside the Removing Obstacles exercise; Pressfield's inventory of Resistance tactics maps almost exactly onto the alibi list you are about to complete.

exercises.title: Goal Achievement Plan — Reflection
exercises.attribution: Personal Strategic Planning™ — © Sam Koshy / Compass Career Life Solutions, Canada.

content_json.intro: Think about each of your top eight goals together as a single Personal Strategic Plan. Answer each of the six questions below. Take into account the outside influences that affect your decisions and plans.

questions.prompt: How important is it that you achieve these goals? Why now?
How do these goals relate to your long-term goals? What conflicts are there between them?
How will you feel when you attain these goals? Try to imagine yourself with the goals achieved — what are your feelings?
How will you feel if you do not attain these goals? Try to imagine again — what are your feelings?
What do you think about your chances of succeeding? What will happen if you do succeed?
What will happen if you fail at one or more of these goals?

exercises.title: Removing Obstacles — Per Goal
exercises.attribution: Personal Strategic Planning™ — © Sam Koshy / Compass Career Life Solutions, Canada.

content_json.intro: For each of your top 8 goals, name up to 4 personal shortcomings and up to 4 external obstacles that could keep you from achieving it. You need not eliminate the block entirely — anything you can do to lessen its force will start you moving toward your goal.

exercises.title: EXERCISE: Success/Failure & Win/Lose — The IF List
exercises.attribution: (From Lar Short's Institute of Total Person Facilitation)

content_json.prompt: The purpose of alibis is to cover up weaknesses. The energy used in supporting alibis is usually more than enough to correct the weakness. Check any alibis YOU have used — this is about honest self-awareness, not judgment.

options.label: If only I didn't have a husband/wife and family
If I had money
If I could get a job
If only I had time
If only my mother/father/wife/husband/lover/children understood me
If only my teacher would really listen
If conditions around me were only different
If I could live my life over again
If only I wasn't a sinner
If I didn't fear what 'they' would say
If I had been given a chance
If I had been given another chance
If I now had a chance
If other people didn't 'have it in for me'
If nothing happens to stop me
If I didn't make excuses and was willing to learn and correct my weaknesses
If I were only younger
If I could only do what I want
If I could only remember my birth/childhood
If my body or appearance was different
If I didn't have so much to do

If I had been born rich
If I could meet the 'right' people

If I only had somebody to help me

If I had enough pull/knew the right people
If I had a good education
If I had good health
If times were better
If only 'it' was easier
If I could just get started
If I had more personality/charisma
If my talents were known
If I could only get out of debt
If I hadn't failed
If everybody didn't oppose me
If I didn't have so many worries
If I could marry the right person
If people weren't so dumb
If my family weren't so extravagant
If I were sure of myself
If I just knew they could see how I see it
If luck were not against me
If I hadn't been born under the wrong star
If it were not true that 'what will be will be'
If I didn't have such bad karma
If I didn't have to work hard
If only the things 'I have to do' didn't make me so tired
If only I had enough energy
If I were in touch with my psychic powers
If I hadn't lost my money
If I lived somewhere else
If I didn't have a past
If I only had a business of my own
If only I could get paid for doing what I want/like to do
If other people would only listen to me
If I hadn't been born
If I could just get a rest
If people weren't so superficial
If life was a little easier
If I could just finish things
If I were more creative
If only my teacher would finally arrive
If I had someone I could really communicate with
If I lived in a big city/small town/the country
If only I were free
If I weren't so fat/skinny
If I could just get a break

exercises.title: My Declaration of Self-Esteem
exercises.attribution: (by Virginia Satir, 1970 — Virginia Satir is known as the grandmother of family therapy)

content_json.prompt:
Read through Virginia Satir's Declaration of Self-Esteem (1970), then write any thoughts or feelings it brings up for you:

"I am me. In all the world, there is no one exactly like me. There are persons who have some parts of me, but no one adds up exactly like me. Therefore, everything that comes out of me is authentically mine because I alone chose it.

I own everything about me — my body, including everything it does; my mind, including all its thoughts and ideas; my eyes, including the images of all they behold; my feelings, whatever they may be — anger, joy, frustration, love, disappointment, excitement; my mouth, and all the words that come out of it; my voice, loud or soft, and all my actions, whether they be to others or to myself.

I own my fantasies, my dreams, my hopes, my fears. I own all my triumphs and successes, all my failures, and mistakes.

Because I own all of me, I can become intimately acquainted with me. By so doing I can love me and be friendly with me in all my parts. I can then make it possible for all of me to work in my best interest.

I know there are aspects about myself that puzzle me, and other aspects I do not know. But as long as I am friendly and loving to myself, I can courageously and hopefully look for solutions to the puzzles and for ways to find out more about me.

I own me, and therefore I can engineer me. I am me and I am okay."

What thoughts or feelings arise in response to this declaration? What parts resonate most deeply with you?
